title: Church Government, Part 9: Deacon Qualifications and the Role of Women in Ministry
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: 1. Review of Deacon Qualifications (1 Timothy 3:8-13)
subpoints: Five moral categories: self-controlled in speech, self-controlled in drinking, not greedy for money, theologically orthodox, and upstanding moral character
The deacon must keep confidences -- ministry brings knowledge of private struggles that must not be shared
Verse 10: must be tested first; the basis for an internship process before ordination
These are not super-Christian requirements but the ordinary expectations for every Christian man

outline.title: 2. Are the Women of 1 Timothy 3:11 Deaconesses?
subpoints: The Greek word gunaikas can mean either 'wives' or 'women' -- context must decide
Arguments against a separate office of deaconess: Paul would not insert a new office between deacon verses 10 and 12
The husband-of-one-wife requirement in verse 12 naturally follows qualifications for the deacon's wife in verse 11
Deaconesses lack a separate testing requirement (verse 10 applies to deacons, not to verse 11)
The better translation is 'their wives' -- most Protestants since the Reformation agree

outline.title: 3. Women Are Called to Deaconal Work
subpoints: The office of deacon is reserved for men, but the work of service is open to all
Women can minister to the sick, dying, mentally and physically impaired; share their faith and resources
Women can counsel, mentor, teach children and other women, lead women's Bible studies, plan missions
The boundary from 1 Timothy 2:12: women should not preach in worship services or teach men with authority

outline.title: 4. Jesus's Revolutionary Ministry to Women
subpoints: Jesus spoke publicly to women when other rabbis would not (Luke 7; John 4; John 8)
He ministered to women who were sick and suffering (Mark 1; Luke 13; Matthew 9; Mark 7)
He received women's service and financial support (Luke 8; Martha and Mary)
Women -- Mary Magdalene and others -- were the first and primary witnesses of the resurrection
Jesus addressed women as 'daughters of Abraham,' placing them on equal spiritual footing with men

outline.title: 5. The Most Important Message: Men Must Stand Up
subpoints: Women in most congregations are more spiritually engaged than men -- this is a rebuke to men, not an excuse
Most 'women's problems' in the church are actually failures of male leadership
When men lead as godly, humble, self-sacrificing husbands, women will happily flourish in their God-given roles
The heartbeat of complementarianism: not 'women sit down' but 'men stand up and be men'

summary:
This Sunday School lesson continues the examination of the office of deacon, beginning with a detailed look at the five moral categories of qualifications in 1 Timothy 3:8-13: self-controlled speech (including confidentiality), self-controlled with regard to drinking, non-covetousness, theological orthodoxy, and upstanding moral character. Pastor Lyro emphasizes that these are not elevated super-Christian standards but the baseline expected of every man who professes faith in Christ.

The lesson then addresses the hotly debated question of whether 1 Timothy 3:11 describes a female deacon, a deaconess as a separate office, or simply the wives of deacons. Pastor Lyro walks through the grammatical evidence and contextual arguments, concluding that the better interpretation is that Paul is speaking of the deacons' wives -- a position held by most Protestant scholars since the Reformation. However, this conclusion affirms rather than diminishes the necessity of women's involvement in deaconal work: the office is reserved for men, but the service itself is open to all.

A substantial portion of the lesson surveys Jesus's revolutionary ministry to women -- his public conversations with women, his healing of women, his willingness to receive their service, and most significantly, his choice of women as the first and primary witnesses of the resurrection. This establishes the basis for understanding women's ministry in the church: not limited by a lack of worth or ability, but shaped by the complementary design of God.

The lesson closes with a challenge to men: women's spiritual engagement outpaces men's in most congregations, and when women have ventured into teaching and authority roles, it has usually been because men abdicated their responsibility. The message of complementarianism must be 'men, stand up' -- not 'women, sit down.'
transcript:
## Church Government, Part 9: Deacon Qualifications and the Role of Women in Ministry

*Pastor Tito Lyro -- Sunday School*

---

Today we have our last lesson on the series on Church Government and I want to start by reading again, First Timothy 3:8 through 13.

> *Likewise, deacons must be reverent, not double-tongued, not given to much wine, not greedy for money, holding the mystery of the faith with a pure conscience. But let these also first be tested, then let them serve as deacons, being found blameless. Likewise, their wives must be reverent, not slanderers, temperate, faithful in all things. Let deacons be the husbands of one wife, ruling their children and their own houses well. For those who have served well as deacons obtain for themselves a good standing and great boldness in the faith which is in Christ Jesus.*

Jesus Christ appointed a government for his church, and that government is comprised of elders and deacons. Both had to be godly men who serve Christ. We see here that both the eldership and the deaconate are to be filled by men -- by males. This is not a human invention or determination. It is what God has appointed in his word. Some people hear that and conclude there is no room for women to serve in the church. Yet nothing that was said in this series teaches that. The office does not dictate the worth of a person.

### Qualifications of the Deacon: Five Moral Categories

The moral qualifications in 1 Timothy 3:8-13 fall into five categories:

**1. Self-controlled in speech.** A deacon doing his work will find out things about the lives of families in the church that could be hurtful and divisive if shared with others. He must be a man who can keep his lips sealed, even when he finds out about struggles in the lives of needy people. This is true of elders as well. The burdens of the office are to be carried by the officer, not their wives.

**2. Self-controlled in the area of drinking/substance use.** The deacon manifests self-control with regard to alcohol and is not given to mind-altering substances. By being self-controlled in this area, the deacon inspires confidence that when money is trusted to him, it won't be misspent.

**3. Not fond of gaining money unlawfully or unethically.** He shows this in his own finances. If he is in charge of handling the tithes and gifts of the congregation, he must be a man you can trust with money. We receive offerings, have three men count them, another man deposit them, and another reconcile with financial software -- about twelve sets of eyes on the whole process, with dual signatures required on every check.

**4. Orthodox in belief** (verse 9): "holding the mystery of the faith with a pure conscience." The deacon is not required to teach like the elders, but is required to hold historic Christian orthodoxy with a clear conscience. God wants mercy to be ministered by people who really believe his word. The theological oath of office for a deacon is almost exactly the same as for an elder.

**5. Upstanding moral character:** a husband of one wife, ruling children and household well -- godliness, not credentials, is the requirement.

### Are the Women of Verse 11 Deaconesses?

The Greek word *gunaikas* in verse 11 can mean either "wives" or "women" -- context must decide. Three interpretations are offered:
- Female deacons (same office as male deacons)
- Deaconesses (a separate, subordinate office)
- Wives of deacons

Arguments that this is *not* an office of deaconess or female deacon:

1. It would be strange for Paul to insert a third office in the middle of instructions for deacons (verse 10 is about deacons, verse 12 is about deacons).
2. The requirement to be "husband of one wife" in verse 12 naturally follows qualifications for the deacon's wife in verse 11.
3. If Paul were giving requirements for female deacons, he would include something about their families and marital status, as he does for male deacons.
4. Deacons must be tested first (verse 10) -- this testing requirement is not applied to the women in verse 11.
5. There are no qualifications for the elder's wife -- that's because the elder's wife cannot share in the functions of teaching and authority. The deacon's wife *can* share in the deaconal work, hence qualifications for her.

Conclusion: Paul is speaking of the wives of the deacons. This has been the majority position among Protestant scholars since the Reformation. But this does not eliminate women from deaconal *work* -- the office is reserved for ordained men, but the service of mercy is open to everyone.

### Women Are Called to Deaconal Work

The office is not open to women by ordination. But the work? Women can do it all. Women can minister to the sick, dying, mentally impaired, and physically handicapped. Women can share their faith and their resources. Women can open their houses to strangers. Women can write, counsel, mentor, organize, plan. Women can pray. Women can serve on church committees. Women can minister to single moms, new moms, cancer survivors, and abuse victims. Women can lead women's Bible studies and teach mathematics and all other subjects to women. Women can plan mission trips and teach children. Women can raise their kids to the glory of God. Women can embrace singleness as a gift from God. Women can do all of this cross-culturally with unreached peoples.

In other words, there are 10,000 things women can do in ministry. They cannot be elders and deacons. If that does not satisfy us, then we have a problem. The boundary from 1 Timothy 2:12: women should not preach in our worship services, nor teach men, nor exercise authority over men. That "or" is important -- Paul says two things: teach *or* exercise authority. These are two things. All teaching in the church is authoritative; the principle covers both.

### Jesus's Revolutionary Ministry to Women

Jesus had a revolutionary ministry to women out of a cultural background that minimized the dignity of women:

- He spoke publicly with women in circumstances other rabbis would not have (Luke 7; John 4; John 8)
- He ministered to the sick and suffering: Peter's mother-in-law (Mark 1), the bent-over woman (Luke 13), the bleeding woman (Matthew 9), the Syrophoenician woman (Mark 7)
- He received women's ministry: women anointed him, women provided financial support (Luke 8), Martha and Mary offered hospitality
- Women -- Mary Magdalene, Joanna, Susanna, and others -- are listed by name as part of his circle
- Most significantly: Jesus chose women as the first and primary witnesses of his resurrection in all four Gospels. A woman's testimony carried no legal weight in the first century, yet the Lord chose this group to testify of the central fact of the faith.
- Jesus addressed women as "daughters of Abraham," placing them on the same spiritual plane as men
- He protected women from being treated as sexual objects (Matthew 5) and as property in divorce law (Matthew 5; 19)

Underlying all of this is Jesus's radical assumption that women have enormous value and purpose -- both ontologically (in their being) and functionally (in their service to the Lord).

### The Most Important Message: Men Must Stand Up

Almost every pastor will tell you that the women in his church are more spiritually minded, more interested in reading the Bible, more eager to grow in their faith, and more open to serving than the men. This is a problem, and it is primarily a *men's* problem.

No doubt, when women have ventured into areas of teaching and authority reserved for men, they often did so not out of rebelliousness but because men had already relinquished their God-given mandate to spiritually lead, protect, and provide. The reversal we see in Genesis 3 -- where Adam was "right there" with Eve the whole time but said nothing -- plays out again and again in the church.

The heartbeat of our message in this issue must not be *women, sit down.* The heartbeat must be *men, stand up and be men.* If men behave and lead as godly, humble, self-sacrificing, biblical men, then women will happily live and flourish in the responsibilities that God has designed for them. Let's pray.
